data.title: Sociedad Quimica Y Minera de Chile: A Buy, But With The Real Upside Pushed Into 2026
data.description:
When I posted my first article about Sociedad Química y Minera de Chile (SQM) back in June, lithium context was clearly negative: prices were decreasing strongly, and the market was already discounting an actually gloomy scenario. Since then, the share has increased by 115%, actually higher than the S&P 500 in the same period.

Later on, during August, I wrote again about SQM with a more cyclical view, and since that second buy, SQM rose about 50%, again exceeding the index.

The sharp rise allows us to understand where we are today. An important part of the adjustment has already been done, and the worst scenario was left behind. Due to this reason, from now on, the thesis changes. It is not about an initial bounce back, but about evaluating how much value the company can still generate when the cycle begins to normalize.

Q3 2025: signs of change, without euphoria

Q3 2025 results do not show a spectacular recovery, but they show something really important in a business like this: the change in trend. SQM has reported revenue of $1.17B, with growth near 9% YoY, and a $0.62 EPS, lower than expected by the market.

What I consider the most relevant was in the operative results. The company has reached its biggest lithium sales volumes in its history, supported by low costs and a solid execution in Atacama. Moreover, realized lithium prices stabilized and showed a modest sequential improvement in Q3, after a prolonged period of weakness.

In the call, the company was actually clear:

During the third quarter, we experienced a more favorable pricing environment for lithium compared with the previous period. Although the market remains highly volatile, we are cautiously optimistic. Our realized average prices increased. And while we expect this positive trend to continue in the fourth quarter, we remain focused on high-quality production, being a reliable supplier, increasing volumes and continuing to advance our cost reduction initiatives. Demand fundamentals remain strong, not only for electric vehicles, but also from energy storage systems, which already account for more than 20% of global lithium demand.

Even though it is mentioned the environment is still volatile, it is seen the demand is being restored. For me, it does not mean the cycle is fully normalized, but it allows us to think the worst moment was left behind.

Valuation

Looking at the short term growth metrics, SQM does not look attractive today, because we see revenues and EBITDA decaying in year-over-year terms, and 2025 is still outlined as a weak year. Nevertheless, even if the Growth Grade is low, it is not due to serious headwinds of the company, but a moment within the cycle with lower prices, still depressed.

When looking altogether, the picture changes. SQM is listed at an EV/EBITDA FWD near 15x. This shows us, even though it is not cheap, it is not at extreme levels. For a more complete outlook, I will compare it to two peers: Albemarle (ALB), its more direct competitor, is listed at higher multiples, whereas Rio Tinto (RIO) is listed lower. I must clarify RIO is not a pure lithium producer, but a diversified mining company that I only use as a scale reference and capital discipline.

What I observe is the difference between SQM and the rest of the sector: the company still shows a superior operative profitability. Its EBITDA margin is around 32%, even in this low section of the cycle. This combination of high margins and low costs explains why the market is still willing to pay a relative premium, even when short term growth is weak.

Growth did not disappear; it's just that it is coming later…

The central point of the thesis today does not take place in 2025; rather, it is based on what begins to be seen since 2026. With maximum volumes, a gradual improvement in prices, and a demand beginning to play in favor, results can react fast when the cycle stops playing against.

Consensus expects EPS to rise strongly in 2026, with an increase of more than 60% YoY. This rebound does not require an extreme scenario, but it is enough with a partial normalization of prices and margins, far from the peaks of the last supercycle, but over the current floor.

To put this idea into numbers, I take as a reference the valuation that the market today looks to discount for SQM at the same time that results are normalized. With an expected EPS for 2026 near to $4 and multiples that tend to compress to more normal levels while the earnings are better, I believe the implicit value of the stock is about $82-$85.

From current levels, that implies a potential increase of 15%-20% for 12-18 months.

Risks that are still present

Risks have not disappeared. The main risk is still the lithium price: a volatile commodity and a slower recovery than expected could delay the results improvement.

Moreover, Chilean regulatory risks can influence the greater competition from Australia and China. Those are real risks and justify a prudent layout.

Conclusion

After the strong rally over the last few months, SQM is not a short term opportunity anymore. I still believe it is a Buy, but a different Buy from some months ago. Today I do not see an unbalanced position, but a cyclical quality investment, with margins over the sector and a direct exposure to the re-improvement of the lithium cycle since 2026.

The market has already recognized an important part of the value, but does not entirely discount the future improvement of the results. Because of that, I keep my Buy rating, but with a more selective approach and a horizon from medium to long term. Not a stock to catch fast movements, but to keep patience while the cycle fully resets.

data.title: QQQH: Tax-Efficient Income That Hedges Downside Risk
data.description:
Overview

One of the biggest issues I've had with option ETFs is the limited protection during pullbacks. As I highlighted in my prior article titled, 'The Blueprint To Navigate Option ETFs Through 2026', I believe it's best to stick with funds that have limited NAV erosion and actually hold the equities that make up the index. The NEOS Nasdaq-100 Hedged Equity Income ETF (QQQH) attempts to address this problem by implementing a strategy that aims to provide greater protection during market declines. Due to the lower yield and more complex strategy, I believe that QQQH doesn't quite get the recognition that it may deserve.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that QQQH's share price has increased by about 3.4%. The fund has demonstrated its ability to participate in the positive market momentum over the last few months. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 13.2% over the same time frame. QQQH now offers investors a starting dividend yield of about 9%, while issuing payouts on a monthly basis. QQQH has demonstrated its ability to keep payouts within a consistent range, while delivering attractive returns over time. Therefore, QQQH remains a solid choice for investors seeking a source of reliable income generation.

As we approach the new year, I wanted to share some insights on why I believe QQQH makes for a solid long-term position. However, there are some caveats here that investors should consider. For instance, the fund is very likely to underperform during market rallies, so QQQH is best implemented as an accompanying position, rather than its own sole holding. Additionally, historical performance makes me question whether or not the hedge strategy may be efficient specifically during periods of prolonged downside movements. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that QQQH implements to generate its income.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, QQQH has total net assets of $353 million that is spread across constituents of the Nasdaq-100 Index. The fund offers a reasonable expense ratio of 0.68%, which may be justified by the higher dividend yield offered to investors. The fund's goal is to offer tax-efficient monthly income from its option writing strategy on the Nasdaq-100. What separates QQQH from peers such as its sibling fund, NEOS NASDAQ-100 High Income ETF (QQQI), is the incorporation of a put spread strategy that limits downside risks.

What I also like about QQQH is the fact that it actually holds common shares of the equities within the index. This means that investors are getting direct exposure to the upside movement of the index. Since the fund is made up of constituents of the Nasdaq-100, we can see that the fund leans most heavily on technology companies. As we can see below, QQQH's technology exposure makes up 54.1% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to communication and consumer cyclical companies, which accounts for 16.52% and 13.47% of assets respectively.

To generate the income needed to support the high yield, QQQH implements an OTM (out-of-the-money) option writing strategy against the index itself, rather than its actual holdings. This allows the fund to directly participate in the growth of its holdings, while also collecting option premiums from the index, which are used to support the higher payouts. Although an OTM option strategy means that the strike price sits above the current market price of the index and some upside is possible, growth is still limited to whatever the selected strike price is. Therefore, QQQH is essentially sacrificing future growth for income now.

Since QQQH is made up of constituents of the index, investors are getting exposure to some of the highest quality growth companies in the world. For instance, NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) is the largest holding with a weight of 9.19%. This is followed by many other notable companies such as Apple Inc. (AAPL), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Broadcom Inc. (AVGO), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG), just to name a few. As investors may already be aware, the Nasdaq-100 is a lot more volatile than the S&P 500, which can be attributed to the larger focus on technology companies.

To mitigate the downside risks of the sector, QQQH implements a put spread strategy. In simple terms, this means that QQQH simply buys put options with a strike price slightly below the current market price of the index. So in a scenario where markets experience a pullback, the value of these put options increases and can offset losses within the portfolio. This is accompanied by another set of put options that are initiated with an even lower strike price, which is considered to be deep OTM.

The spread between these two put zones are what creates the ultimate buffer for QQQH. So as long as the market pulls back somewhere between the range of these two puts, QQQH is capitalizing on a decline and offset some losses. While this can help offset losses, it may not completely prevent QQQH from experiencing losses. It ultimately comes down to how severe of a pullback the market experiences. Lastly, QQQH also implements a secondary option strategy to generate income from the S&P 500, with the sole purpose of offsetting the costs associated with the put collar spread strategy. The following strategy is laid out as follows:

1) holds a long position indexed to the S&P 500 Index; 2) on a monthly basis buys a 2.5% – 5% S&P 500 Index (SPX) put option spread; and 3) sells a monthly out-of-the-money (OTM) SPX call option to cover the cost of the put spread.

So unlike the typical buy-write fund, QQQH's strategy is more complex because it actively puts in the effort to minimize downside for investors. Combined with the fact that QQQH directly holds common shares of its equities, this makes it a more efficient long-term holding compared to some other option ETFs that track the Nasdaq-100.

Performance Insights & Risks

Despite the fund offering direct exposure to so many high quality large-cap growth companies, I believe that investors should limit their expectations around the fund's ability to provide capital appreciation over time. To demonstrate this weakness, we can refer to the fund's performance since its inception. As we can see below, QQQH's share price has increased by 8.5% since inception. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 75.8% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that most of their returns will come from the fund's ability to continue paying out a high rate of distributions over time.

As I previously mentioned, QQQH's strategy for protecting the downside movement works as long as the market pullback isn't severe and lands in the 'buffer zone'. If the pullback is rapid and severe, QQQH will fail to capitalize on the downside. A good example of this can be demonstrated over the course of 2022. Market indices experienced rapid double-digit declines due to uncertainty around Covid at the time, rapid interest rate hikes, a shifting labor market, and record-breaking inflationary data.

As we can see below, QQQH suffered from a large price decline of 33.74% through 2022. The pullback was so large for the fund that the share price eroded more than a traditional index-tracking ETF, such as the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ), over the same period. When including the distributions received from QQQH, the strategy did help the fund see slightly smaller total losses compared to QQQ. However, the share price erosion is difficult to recover from since the upside growth is still capped by written options. So in a scenario where the market pullback is deep, QQQH may not do a good job at limiting losses with this put spread approach.

Lastly, the biggest risk to taking a position in QQQH is the probability of underperformance. I am bullish on the outlook of the Nasdaq-100 due to the projected growth of the AI market. For instance, Precedence Research estimates that the size of the AI market will increase at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 19.20% through 2034. Since QQQH caps upside growth, the fund isn't the best way to participate in the expansion of the sector.

Since QQQH directly provides exposure to the common shares of the equities of the index, the fund is aligned to directly participate in the growth. Although the growth is capped, long-term investors can still get exposure to the expansion of the AI sector in some capacity. My summarized thesis is that AI will help companies significantly improve operating margins by reducing costs. This has the potential to directly translate to earnings growth for many of the large-cap companies that QQQH provides exposure to.

When measuring QQQH's performance against QQQ, this weakness is already very visible. Investors need to understand that a high dividend yield doesn't automatically equate to a high total return over time. Since the fund's inception, QQQH's total return sits at 75.85%. While this is solid for an income fund, it severely underperforms QQQ's total return of nearly 204% over the same time frame. So if you are a younger investor that doesn't have a need for income generation at this time, you may be better off simply holding QQQ. The income focus of QQQH makes it more efficient as an accompanying position if you already hold a diverse portfolio of assets.

I believe the only scenario where QQQH won't underperform QQQ is if sentiment within the technology sector shifts. If the Nasdaq's momentum were to shift and the index traded sideways, the additional income that QQQH captures could help the fund outperform QQQ when including all dividends paid. This would reinforce the idea that QQQH is appropriate as a hedge against the uncertainty around technology's future. Sentiment can change very quickly, with one of the latest challenges being the large amount of capital being allocated to capex spending with many of these large-cap companies.

Dividend Utility

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.4062 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 9%. Although the fund has demonstrated its ability to keep payouts within a consistent range, investors should be aware that payouts are likely to remain variable for the lifetime of the fund. Each payout is determined by factors such as the success of the written options, overall momentum of the market, and implied volatility of its holdings. Therefore, payouts will adjust through different market cycles. As we can see below, payouts have trended upwards over the last year as the Nasdaq-100 continued to rally higher.

Therefore, I would advise caution during a scenario where market momentum shifts. If we experience a prolonged pullback, there is a very good chance that the declared payouts will eventually shrink over time. A good instance of this was demonstrated during the March and April period of 2025. As market indices declined in response to President Trump's tariff proposals, QQQH's payouts started to decline. Therefore, QQQH's income potential may decline if sentiment of the index shifts and growth slows.

On a more positive note, QQQH has made the effort to prioritize issuing distributions in a tax-efficient manner. For instance, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that 100% of the distributions paid YTD were fueled by return of capital. Unlike net investment income, return of capital distributions aren't classified as income and therefore, aren't taxed as such. Instead, a return of capital distribution reduces an investor's cost basis and allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale. So for investors with no intention to sell, this structure provides flexibility in terms of which accounts for a position in QQQH can be utilized in.

However, the method for payouts can change based on market conditions. There may be times where distributions are supported by net investment income, which is classified as ordinary dividends and have the highest tax burden for investors. Therefore, it's good to remain cautious about the possibility that some income received from QQQH will still produce tax consequences for investors.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that QQQH is a buy at this time for investors that are bullish on the outlook of the Nasdaq-100. If my thesis is correct and the Nasdaq-100 continues to expand through 2026, QQQH is aligned to generate tax-efficient income while still participating in the upside. Additionally, QQQH provides direct exposure to some of the highest quality growth companies in the world, while issuing payouts in a tax-efficient nature. The focus on tax-efficient distributions means that QQQH can be utilized outside of tax-advantaged accounts with very little tax consequences.

The put spread strategy helps the fund provide a high income with some downside protection against mild pullbacks. Although I believe the index will ultimately grow, the ride forward is likely to be volatile, so the hedged approach is an efficient way to protect yourself. While I expect QQQH to underperform a more traditional ETF such as QQQ over the long term due to the focus on income, I think that there's always a chance for QQQH to outperform QQQ if the sentiment shifts. If the Nasdaq-100 trades flat over the next year, the additional income received from QQQH may help the fund outperform during periods of uncertainty.

data.title: A Check-Up On The Screws Holding The Economy Together
data.description:
In our July 2025 article (here) when we last checked on the economic screws, we stated…

So far, tariffs have been repeatedly delayed — only around $20B/month has been collected — and net money-creation continues at a higher rate than last year. Although the BBB spending bill removes resources from the poor and gives them to the rich, the increased deficit-spending ($5T over 10-years) should support the stock market and keep the primary bull trend in place over the next year.

Six-months later, the tariffs have been running at twice what they were in July (~$40B/month) and DOGE has been disbanded with an estimate of ~$200B of cuts (here), far short of the $2T Musk predicted — since Government spending at this point is slightly higher year-over-year, the $200B in cuts must have been applied to other budget lines.

The BBB (Big Beautiful Bill) does not come into effect until the New Year and will bring tax-cuts of $4.5T over ten years with $2.3T of those benefits — more than half — going to the top 10% of earners (here). Tax cuts are the same as spending increases because they leave more money in the private sector, but if these tax cuts are balanced off by reduced spending in areas such as healthcare insurance subsidies, then the positive effect on the economy could be blunted.

These uncertainties make predicting the next 6-12 months in the economy much less certain, but in this article we will check on the current state of the "screws" that hold the economy together.

The Screws Holding the Economy Together

The first chart below is the BaR grid (from econ p.i.) dated June 27, 2025 and the second chart is the most recent dated December 19, 2025.

Notice that the MoC (mean of coordinates) has moved out of the expansion quadrant and into, while the WMoC (weighted mean of coordinates) and the LD (leading indicators) have both weakened their positioning. This means that the average of economic measures have weakened and are no longer growing. This screw is a little loose.

The GDPNow estimate of Q2 2025 is averaging 3.5% (compared to 2.5% last July). This screw is tight (chart below).

Industrial Production of the United States has increased since the start of 2025. This screw is tight (chart below).

Capacity utilization either stalls or drops ahead of recessions. However, it is a necessary condition, not a sufficient condition for a recession; no recessions resulted following the drop in capacity utilization in 1985, 1995, 1997, and 2015. Capacity utilization has been decreasing, making this screw looser than we would like to see.

PCE (personal consumption expense), which is the Fed's preferred gauge of inflation, has been steady since June. This screw is tight enough (chart below).

The University of Michigan consumer sentiment for the US has been trending lower since July. This screw is loose.

The Aruoba-Diebold-Scotti business conditions index is designed to track real business conditions at high frequency. The average value of the ADS index is zero. Bigger positive values indicate progressively better-than-average conditions, and bigger negative values indicate progressively worse than average conditions. The index shows that business conditions are still slightly worse than average, but have improved recently (green arrow) which makes it a tightening economic screw (chart below).

Initial jobless claims [blue] rise for a year ahead of recessions, while business activity [red] falls (chart below).

Since 2022, initial claims and business activity have been stable. These are both tight economic screws (chart below).

The Manufacturing PMI for the US continues to grow (above 50), but at a reduced rate over the past three months. This screw remains tight enough (chart below).

The Services PMI continues to grow (above 50), but at a reduced rate over the past three months. This screw is still tight enough, but getting looser (chart below).

Banks tightening lending-standards always precedes recessions (red-lines below), but tightening lending-standards does not always lead to recessions (purple-lines below); e.g., 1994-1999 and 2014-2016, and 2021-2022. Lending-standards continue to be relaxed (green-line below). This screw is tight.

Recessions occur when loan delinquency-rates are rising, and when Household debt service as a percent of disposable income becomes elevated. Currently, both measures are just above historically low levels. This screw is tight.

Recessions are always preceded by at least two-years of falling housing starts. Housing starts have been falling since 2022. This screw is loose.

Truck transport employment (blue-line below) stops growing or declines, and heavy truck sales (red-line) collapse for more than a year ahead of recessions — the exception being 2015-2017 (pink-rectangle below). Truck transport employment has come down from the all-time highs of 2023 (blue-highlight) and truck sales have dropped below the up-trend line (black). This is a dangerously loose economic screw.

Corporate profits tend to decline (red), sometimes for years, ahead of recessions. Profits continue to rise from the pandemic lows. This is a tight screw.

The Most Important Screw of All

Money drives both the economy and the stock market, but most people simply do not understand what money is or where it comes from.

Money can be anything that we agree is capable of releasing real-resources, like labor and materials. It is not a physical entity, and today's money is not fixed to anything physical; Richard Nixon took the US dollar off the gold-standard in 1971. The US dollar is created by Congressional spending-laws, and is destroyed by taxation: by the way, taxation is what forces everyone in the US to use the US dollar as currency. Taxpayers need to earn US dollars before they can pay their taxes. Taxpayers cannot create US dollars — that would be counterfeiting. All US dollars are created by Government spending. The Federal Government is "self-funding."

Net spending is the difference between nominal-spending and taxation. If the Government spends (creates) more US dollars than it taxes back, then we say it has a deficit; it has deposited more money in private bank accounts than it has taxed back.

Government deficit = private surplus

Government deficits correlate positively with the SPX (chart below). This makes sense, since bigger economies (and higher stock markets) require more money in the system.

So far, the fiscal-2026 (started October 1, 2025) annualized run-rates of the deficit and of the net money-creation (which includes bank credit creation) are lower than in any year since 2022 (chart below).

This is a loose screw, but it is still early in the fiscal-year and the BBB takes effect in the new calendar-year with a potential $5T increase to the money supply (by way of tax-cuts), and if the tariff taxes are stopped or (better yet) refunded, then the improved fund-flows would tighten the "deficit-spending screw" which would support the economy and the stock market.

In conclusion, 60% of the economic screws are tight, and as long as the BBB's tax-cuts take effect in January, many of the loose screws will tighten — starting with net money-creation — making a recession in the next 6-12 months unlikely. However, if the deficit and net money-creation continue to slow, then the economy and the stock market will stall and recession and a bear market become more likely.

Investors can stay long the stock market through technology ETFs such as SOXX, AIQ and XT, as well as broad spectrum ETFs such as SPY and QQQ.

data.title: PepsiCo: Offering Value Amid Domestic Struggles
data.description:
If you can call them right, buying into blue chip turnaround stories can be a lucrative strategy. The idea is that established corporations typically enjoy that bit more room to sort out whatever issues are weighing on them. If they do, then not only will you get the profit growth, but those profits often go on to win a higher multiple from the market as well. A positive double whammy of sorts.

That's broadly how I see PepsiCo (PEP). Despite rallying over 15% since July, this was off a multi-year low. Its share price is still around 20% lower than it was three years back, as declining volume, contracting profit margins, and elevated capital spending have all weighed on sentiment.

While the company has its issues, particularly at home, I come back to what I said above about blue chips and wiggle room, and PepsiCo is the bluest of the blue. With the stock's multiples looking cheap relative to past marks, I believe these shares still offer value at today's price.

When PFNA Sneezes...

For years, the market accepted that PepsiCo's North American operations consisted of a poorly performing vertically integrated drinks unit (PepsiCo Beverages North America, or PBNA for short) offset by a much stronger foods business (PepsiCo Foods North America; PFNA for short). In turn, PFNA is made up of a high-powered snacks division (Frito-Lay) and the much weaker breakfast-oriented Quaker Foods.

With the above in mind, PBNA's travails aren't news. Yes, its performance has been weak this year - volumes fell 3% through the first three quarters, while core operating profit of $2.6 billion was basically flat - but this is part of a long-term trend. In real terms, PBNA's profits have fallen over the past decade.

While PFNA's past growth has been much better, recent trends haven't been so good. Indeed, after years of consecutive growth, volumes at Frito-Lay fell 2.5% last year. PFNA's volumes have continued to head south in 2025, also falling by around 2.5% in the first three quarters.

Lackluster growth is only part of the story, though. Economics matter even more. Frito's (and by extension, PFNA's) are excellent, with its return on assets averaging around nine times PBNA's between 2015 and 2023 (N.B. PepsiCo ceased reporting the divisional breakdown of its assets from 2024).

Because it is so much more profitable, a dollar of extra profit from PFNA is worth more to PepsiCo's stockholders than a dollar from PBNA. This dynamic enticed management to invest heavily in its foods business in recent years, partly to service volume growth that hasn't turned up. Indeed, while PFNA's organic revenue fell 2% through the first three quarters, segmental depreciation rose nearly 20%. On the back of this, core operating profit fell around 8%.

Put simply, PepsiCo is particularly sensitive to developments in its domestic foods business, Frito specifically. When that unit sneezes, the whole company catches a cold. I believe this is why PepsiCo stock now trades for 18.5x trailing-four-quarter core EPS, down from 23.7x at the start of 2023.

It's Not All Bad

While PepsiCo has work to do to get its domestic foods business back on track, at least it is starting from a position of strength. Specifically, Frito commands a whopping 40%-plus share of the North American savory snacks market based on retail sales. Not only that, but products like Doritos and Lay's have a welcome habit of turning over very quickly. That's important because grocery retailers typically operate on very thin margins, and they have to offset this by generating more sales per dollar of invested capital. That makes PepsiCo a valuable partner for the industry, affording it a lot of latitude.

Also, let's not forget PepsiCo's vast international operations, the entirety of which has contributed over $5 billion to firm-wide core operating income so far in 2025. In contrast to its domestic performance, that was 7% higher than the same period last year. At 19.8%, the associated margin was up 95bps year-on-year, and it was 350bps higher compared to 2018.

Finally, in response to activist pressure, PepsiCo's management has acknowledged the need for spending discipline. I mentioned in the previous section that management had ramped up investment spending in recent years. Last year, PepsiCo spent the equivalent of just over 13% of its sales on CapEx, marketing and R&D (my definition of investing for consumer staples companies). This was around 180bps higher than a decade ago, most of which has come from higher CapEx.

PepsiCo's trailing-four-quarter CapEx-to-sales ratio is around 5.4%. Management has committed to spending less than 5% in 2026. This looks very reasonable. According to a recent company presentation, over 25% of last year's bill was spent on growth initiatives. So, I think the company has scope to lower its capital spending without hurting its business, and this will boost its free cash flow.

There is room to cut costs as well. Since 2018, core corporate unallocated expenses have risen 10bps to 1.9% of sales. Those costs will include things like the HR expenses at PepsiCo's New York headquarters. I would have expected to see much stronger cost leverage over this line. I believe there is fat to be trimmed here, and management seems to agree. In addition to lowering CapEx, they have committed to reducing operating costs, aiming to plow a portion of the savings back into demand creation initiatives to support its core brands.

Valuation

While PepsiCo's domestic problems are real, its shares also appear reasonably valued. For example, PepsiCo's trailing dividend yield is currently around 3.8%, roughly 90bps above its 10-year average.

PepsiCo has grown its dividend at a 7.5% clip since 2010. The way I look at it, if that figure drops by 200bps over the next 15 years, then all else equal, PepsiCo's total annual shareholder return rate would still be above 9%. I consider that attractive for this type of defensive company.

PepsiCo's price-to-earnings ratio also looks relatively cheap. Currently, shares trade for 18.5x trailing-four-quarter core EPS. That's around 15% below their 2015-2024 average (~22x).

Another way I like to think about valuation is to estimate what percentage of a company's market value can be justified by its current earnings. For PepsiCo, that process begins with a trailing-four-quarter core operating profit of $14.3 billion. Less 20% tax, that's roughly $11.4 billion in cash profits. If I discount that at 7.5%, then subtract net debt (~$43 billion), I get $110 billion. That's equal to around 55% of PepsiCo's current market cap. For reference, the comparable figure at the start of 2023 was around 37%.

All in all, I like where PepsiCo's valuation is.

Risks

I think one big risk facing PepsiCo is that its domestic demand problems are structural in nature, increasing health consciousness among consumers, for example. As well as lowering its growth profile, this could permanently degrade the economics of its business, justifying a lower valuation.

Conclusion

There's no doubt that PepsiCo is facing operational challenges in its domestic business. Nobody really expected much from the drinks unit but, given its profitability and expected growth, weakness in the foods business is a cause for concern. While management gets to work sorting that out, PepsiCo is at least starting from a position of strength, while it can also lean on better trends abroad. With its shares looking cheap relative to past marks, I rate them a "Buy."

data.title: XHB Over ITB: Diversification Is Your Best Defense Against Builder Risk
data.description:
The housing market in the US is currently navigating two opposite forces: still high mortgage rates since 2022, and the growth in demand driven by demographic shifts. In this environment, the market remains interesting to invest in. In this comparative analysis, I will look at the two largest ETFs of this industry: the State Street® SPDR® S&P® Homebuilders ETF (XHB) and the iShares U.S. Home Construction ETF (ITB).

XHB earns a buy rating as I think that the fund’s diversified approach to the markets, not mostly relying on construction but capturing all of the supply chain will be the winning positioning after the recent period that was marked by a strong pricing power of homebuilders leveraging inventory scarcity in the market. ITB only earns a hold rating, the fund is much more focused on its key holdings, large builders, which has been a less remunerating positioning lately, and I expect this underperformance will continue as land prices are increasing and that cost considerations are increasingly important, potentially putting margins under further pressure.

Diversification Vs. Concentration

While the two funds are effectively aimed at the housing sector, they are taking two very different routes to do so.

XHB is tracking the S&P Homebuilders Select Industry Index, capturing the performance of companies of the S&P Total Market Index that are classified as "homebuilding" by the GICS framework. To build the index, S&P Global is using an equal-weight methodology, allowing for a stronger presence of small companies: mid-caps account for 42.56% of the fund and small-caps for 4.38% of it. The fund holds 35 companies with a 34.52% top 10 concentration, a prime example of a diversified ETF. This almost eliminates single company risk, while this is one of the key risks of ITB.

Looking at the key holdings of XHB is a clear way of showing both the diversification, but also the fact that XHB has a broad reach. The three largest holdings of the fund are Wayfair Inc. (W), Johnson Controls International plc (JCI), and Williams-Sonoma, Inc. (WSM) at 3.70%, 3.49%, and 3.47%. Clearly, this is enough to see that XHB is not a “homebuilder” only fund, it is aimed at the whole housing value chain, beyond construction, including renovations, furnishing, and upgrades. In addition, other companies such as Carrier Global Corporation (CARR) or Advanced Drainage Systems, Inc. (WMS) are a way to get a diversified exposure where you will not only be exposed to mortgage rates but also see the effects of the demand from infrastructure and climate-control demand.

If XHB is a good example of a diversified fund, ITB is the complete opposite. The fund is tracking the Dow Jones U.S. Select Home Construction Index, capturing the performance of companies included in the “Dow Jones U.S. Broad Stock Market Index that are classified in the DJICS Home Construction, Building Material & Fixtures, Furnishings and Home Improvement Retailers subsectors” with a market-cap weighting, naturally increasing the concentration at the top and increasing the exposure to large caps (65.17% of the fund vs. 53% for XHB). ITB holds 46 companies, which is more than XHB, but with a 65.43% top 10 concentration, of which 31.71% in the top 3: D.R. Horton, Inc. (DHI), PulteGroup, Inc. (PHM), and Lennar Corporation (LEN). DHI alone accounts for 14.70% of the fund, which is what I had in mind when I mentioned the single company risk of ITB. Now, this is not necessarily a bad thing given that this is a strong company, but buying ITB and its high concentration level is a statement that you are convinced about the fact that the trend seen in 2023 and 2024 with increasing market shares for large-cap homebuilders will continue to be sustained. This is not necessarily a bet that I am willing to take, especially as it doesn’t translate to a clear outperformance and because the regulatory scrutiny on consolidating markets is increasing. The fund does include companies such as The Sherwin-Williams Company (SHW) and The Home Depot, Inc. (HD), but their impact in the fund’s performance is offset by the large weights given to the key companies, and the sectoral split diversity is not following.

The Argument For The Supply Chain

One of the reasons why I prefer XHB is because of the way it is capturing value along the full lifecycle of the home, not only relying on construction, as you can see from the table below:

Sector

XHB

ITB

Homebuilding

49.07%

64.15%

Building Products

33.77%

17.20%

Home Improvement Retail

6.67%

10.51%

Homefurnishing Retail

7.18%

0.13%

Home Furnishings

3.31%

1.92%

Specialty Chemicals

0.00%

4.58%

Construction Materials

0.00%

1.44%

You can think of XHB as a "picks and shovels" fund. There is a large allocation in Building Products, almost two times the one of ITB, and I think this is relevant because it provides you with a way of hedging the volatility of new housing starts. Companies in the Building Products segment are providing crucial components (HVAC, insulation, roofing, etc…), making it still indirectly exposed to new housing starts but also to the maintenance industry, which is highly relevant as the housing stock in the US is aging. I believe that we are going to see a "Renovation Renaissance", with a surge of repairs and remodeling, and XHB is perfectly positioned to benefit from it.

In addition, XHB is more exposed to the Home furnishing Retail segment, which is a high beta segment that could prove to be a source of upside while ITB also has a retail exposure but through home improvement warehouses. XHB gets you more exposure to discretionary spending, which is very relevant in a soft landing scenario with growth attached to consumer confidence and wages against pure exposure to housing starts like ITB.

As mentioned, ITB can be seen as a Homebuilding focused fund. This is not necessarily a bad positioning, and it has been very relevant in an environment with housing inventory being squeezed. However, now that cost is an increasing consideration, margins are under pressure and ITB will only outperform if the velocity of new building contracts is sustained. This might happen, but this is not a bet that I am really going to take. There is something unique about ITB’s exposure to Specialty Chemicals (a fancy wording for paint companies) and Construction Materials, but these segments are too niche and underrepresented to really make a difference against the exposure to Homebuilding.

Pricing The Recovery

As XHB and ITB are close competitors, they are aligned in terms of pricing. However, I do think that it is surprising to see XHB showing a 0.35% expense ratio, lower than ITB’s 0.38% despite the fact that XHB’s AUM is $1.7 billion, smaller than ITB’s $2.6 billion. In terms of liquidity, the two funds are really close, with a median bid/ask spread of 0.04% for XHB and 0.03% for ITB. In both cases, if you are worried about liquidity, XHB and ITB are good options.

Looking at valuations, ITB is trading with a P/E ratio of 14.87x and a P/B ratio of 1.97x against 1.06x and 2.00x for XHB. I do see the fact that the P/B ratios are aligned but that the P/E ratio of ITB is lower as a sign that the market is pricing the now lower enthusiasm about homebuilders, and highlighting the relevance of a diversified approach. In addition, I don’t necessarily think that XHB is more expensive because the industrial and retail sectors of the fund are bringing quality and resilient earnings, which is naturally something that has a price. The companies that are selling products are priced at higher points than homebuilders because they are holding intellectual property moats, while the homebuilder activity can be “easily” replicated (i.e. lower barriers to entry).

Finally, the dividends are giving a small edge to XHB with a yield of 0.76%, 0.13 percentage points above ITB’s 0.63%. This is a secondary consideration to the performance, mainly capital gains, that we are going to address below.

Historical Performance And Risk Analysis

Let’s start by taking a look at total returns.

As you can see, XHB has been an outperformer in the short to medium term (all time frames below 5Y), while ITB has been more relevant over the very long term (all time frames above 10Y). From this, we can say that XHB’s diversified strategy is working better in times of volatility and uncertainty, while ITB is relevant when the homebuilding market is strong. If we return to an environment with inventories under pressure, strong housing starts and low pressure on margins, ITB will be the best fund again, but for now, the positioning of XHB wins.

Even in the long term, XHB has always provided investors with a lower volatility than ITB, even if it remains at a high point. We measure it with standard deviations: 27.90% (3 years), 27.48% (5 years), and 26.50% (10 years) for XHB against 30.12% (3 years), 29.64% (5 years), and 29.01% (10 years) for ITB. The consequence of the higher returns and lower volatility metrics of XHB are stronger risk-adjusted returns on the 3Y and 5Y time frames, and 10Y metrics actually show us that the outperformance of ITB is not as impressive as it seems. We measure this with Sharpe ratios: 0.66 (3 years), 0.51 (5 years), and 0.50 (10 years) for XHB against 0.61 (3 years), 0.48 (5 years), and 0.54 (10 years) for ITB.

You can argue that the difference in Sharpe ratios is not very impressive, but sharpe ratios are not taking capital preservation into account. Here, XHB is ahead again, with a maximum drawdown of -35.89% over the last five years and -36.35 over the last ten years against -36.82% and -38.92% for ITB.

The Case For A Broad Recovery

I believe that the macroeconomic environment is now establishing a case for breadth against concentration, as the housing deficit is still the norm, but the implications of this deficit are shifting.

As a wide population of Americans is now locked with mortgages below 4%, the housing mobility is reaching historic lows. This is a fact favoring the Building Products and Home Improvement sectors that you will find more heavily represented in XHB’s portfolio, as the impossibility to move is fueling the will to improve (increase size, upgrade amenities, or replace roofs). For companies such as Masco Corp and Owens Corning, this is an especially dynamic environment with thriving remodeling growth.

We saw during the pandemic that supply chain exposed companies were a source of pain, but the current dynamic is favoring margin expansion for such companies, notably for industrial sub-sectors that are benefiting from a stabilization of input costs but a still important pricing power. This implies that there is a potential for further improvements of margins, even with limited revenue growth. At the other end, homebuilders at the end of the supply chain are not benefiting from the current environment, as for them, it means increased land costs and shortages in the labor market. This is why I like the broad reach of XHB better.

Finally, the high-interest rate environment could be sustained for longer than expected, as hinted by the fact that long term interest rates are not going down despite recent cuts. A return to the pre-COVID zero interest rate environment is not going to happen anytime soon, and this limits the pricing power of homebuilders. ITB’s holdings are less flexible in such an environment, while the retail and furnishing exposure of XHB can leverage changes in consumer employment and purchasing power, moving faster than mortgage rates.

Risks

The primary risk that could affect both XHB and ITB is a potential increase in inflation following lowered short-term interest rates, as the inflation is proving to be stickier than expected. The consequence could be an increased mortgage rate that would interrupt the new construction and remodeling activity.

More specific to XHB, an appeal of the fund is that the fund is exposed to retail themes, and it allows for a better diversification against risks driven by mortgage rates, but this also introduces new risks if discretionary spending were to decrease. A recessionary environment in the US or increased unemployment metrics would hurt furniture sales more than new home contracts, making XHB a worse investment than ITB. In the soft landing scenario currently unfolding, XHB wins, but in a “hard” landing scenario, ITB would be hurt less.

Conclusion

My buy rating for the State Street® SPDR® S&P® Homebuilders ETF is rooted in the fact that the equal weight methodology is more effective at capturing the whole “house” value chain (building products and retail), making it a strong ETF to benefit from a potential "Renovation Renaissance" induced by Americans locked in sub 4% mortgage rates that could happen if the soft landing scenario happens. In front of that, I only give a hold rating to the iShares U.S. Home Construction ETF because it's positioning is weakened by still high-interest rates, and pressures on construction costs that are hurting homebuilders.

data.title: PayPal: A Re-Rating Is Inevitable (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

In early 2024, I initiated coverage on fintech giant - PayPal Holdings, Inc. (PYPL) - with a Buy rating in the mid-$60s:

Based on our fundamental, quantitative, technical, and valuation analysis, PayPal is a solid investment opportunity at ~12x forward P/E and not a value trap as many market pundits believe. Now, PayPal is no Meta [of late-2022], but investors buying in here could reasonably expect to generate 20%+ CAGR return in PYPL stock.

Key Takeaway: I rate PayPal a "Buy" in the $60s, with a strong preference for staggered accumulation via a 3-6 month DCA plan.

Source: The PayPal Dilemma: Value Trap Horror Or Investment Bliss?

Now, while this contrarian call worked like a charm in 2024 [PayPal stock surged over 40%+ from our initial entry point within 12 months], the bet has soured this year as PayPal stock has roundtripped all the way back into the low-$60s/high-$50s -- diverging from the broader equity market, i.e., S&P 500 (SPY), which has continued scaling new all-time highs in recent months:

In today's note, we reassess PayPal's latest business trends and valuation to formulate an informed investment decision.

PayPal: By The Numbers

Jeff Bezos [Amazon (AMZN) founder] has famously said -

The stock is not the company, and the company is not the stock.

And this quote fits PayPal like a hand to glove.

While its recent stock performance has been terrible, PayPal's business has never been larger or more profitable than it is right now!

For Q3 2025, PayPal reported a double beat -

  • Revenues: $8.42B [+7% y/y (acceleration), vs. est. $8.24B].
  • Normalized EPS: $1.34 per share [+12% y/y, vs. est. $1.21 per share].

Now, PayPal's platform usage frequency (measured by the number of transactions) was down -5% y/y [primarily due to management's pricing actions within PayPal's PSP (payment service provider) business]; however, PayPal's total payment volume grew +8% y/y to a record $458B and transaction margin dollars rose +6% y/y to $3.9B in Q3 2025 - powered by healthy growth across the platform - Branded Experiences, Venmo, PSP, and debit cards.

Consequently, PayPal generated $1.7B in free cash flow [$2.3B in adj. free cash flow], out of which it returned $1.5B to shareholders via stock buybacks.

Additionally, PayPal's management raised its FY25 outlook for transaction margin dollars to $15.45-$15.55B and non-GAAP EPS to $5.35-5.39 per share:

That said, PayPal's management did flag macro-induced weakness in consumer spending observed in early Q4 2025, which explains PayPal's softish +2-5% y/y volume growth outlook for the ongoing quarter.

Now, PayPal's near-term business outlook is mired in uncertainty; however, ongoing momentum within Venmo and BNPL, coupled with PayPal's next-gen growth vectors - PayPal World, Agentic Commerce (partnerships with OpenAI, Google, Mastercard, and Perplexity in place), Ads, Crypto, and banking (if approved) - can continue to drive mid-single digit growth at the fintech for years to come.

As per consensus estimates, PayPal's top-line growth rate is projected to tick up in 2026 and 2027, with earnings growth primed to exceed sales growth.

PYPL Stock Is Deeply Discounted

With its market capitalization sitting at ~$56B [$59.86 per share], PayPal is trading at a bargain ~8-9x 2025E FCF of $6-7B, i.e., FCF yield of ~11-12.5%.

And, on an earnings basis, PayPal is trading at ~11x forward EPS, versus the S&P-500, which is trading at ~23x heading into 2026.

Now, bears would quickly point to PayPal's modest, single-digit top-line growth rate as a justification for its lowly trading multiples and apparent relative valuation discount; however, PayPal's ~5% y/y growth rate for 2025 is higher than the S&P-500's median y/y growth rate.

Furthermore, PayPal looks like a bargain even on an absolute basis.

Using conservative assumptions for future growth [5-yr sales CAGR: 5%] and margins [optimized FCF margin: 20%], TQI's Valuation model pegs PayPal's fair value at $91.4 per share [$88B market cap], i.e., ~53% higher from current levels.

Now, predicting where a stock will trade in the short term is impossible; however, over the long run, a stock will track its business fundamentals and obey the immutable laws of money. If the interest rates were to return to artificially low levels (i.e., ZIRP), higher equity multiples would be justifiable. However, I work with the assumption that interest rates will eventually track the long-term average of ~5%. Inverting this number, we get a trading multiple of ~20x (P/FCF).

Assuming a discounted exit multiple of ~15x P/FCF [to bake in PayPal's paltry growth profile], I see PayPal's stock trading at ~$206 per share five years from now. This price target implies a five-year CAGR return of ~28% and handily exceeds our investment hurdle rate of 15%.

In light of PayPal's steep year-to-date decline, PYPL's 5-year expected CAGR return has improved to ~28%, rendering it a Strong Buy under TQI's valuation process.

Concluding Thoughts

PayPal's cocktail of mid-to-high single-digit top line growth, transaction & operating margin expansion, low starting valuation, and a humongous capital return program [~10% stock buyback yield [TTM], freshly initiated quarterly dividend] is a recipe for robust shareholder returns over the next several years. Consequently, I am upgrading PayPal stock to a Strong Buy here.

Key Takeaway: I rate PayPal a Strong Buy in the high-$50s/low-$60s.

Thank you for reading, and happy investing! Please share any questions, thoughts, and/or concerns in the comments section below.

data.title: Power Solutions International: Valuation Has Reset, The Thesis Is Improving
data.description:
Power Solutions International, Inc. (PSIX) has turned into a cautious Buy amidst a backdrop of a significant valuation reset (despite strong Q3 earnings and topline beat) and confirmation of growth signals, primarily led by its power systems and data center demand. When I rated PSIX a Hold in September, I was mostly wary of the valuations. I was also looking for sustained signals of growth and income. Growth and income trends did come up strongly in Q3 2025, but the gross margins trend is still an open concern.

Revisiting The Thesis

One of the core ideas around the Hold rating in September was the fact that I found merit in the narrative of PSIX’s pivot from industrial engines to data-center power systems. The issue was with market's front-running validation, treating early traction signs almost like durable demand. The pivot's legitimacy remains intact, but the story has not progressed to the next stage of proof of concept. Not that I was expecting material progress within a quarter, but the fact remains that the stage of the company (with respect to the data center specific pivot) has not moved materially. However, some of the trends appear more confirmed now.

Since the original thesis, PSIX has delivered a second consecutive quarter of strong revenue and earnings growth, with management explicitly citing robust data center demand and capacity expansion. This provides early evidence that the Q2 breakout was not a one-off event. However, while demand momentum is increasingly credible, visibility into backlog durability or contract cadence remains limited.

The second quarter of outsized revenue growth does alter the Hold view, with fundamental progress being made (although still not sustained detailed evidence of a step change in the company's operating environment due to data center demand). Revenues were up 62% YoY in Q3 FY2025, ticking the multi-quarter revenue growth criteria I had on my checklist (as stated in my previous thesis).

Interestingly, the revenue growth was achieved despite softness in industrial and transportation segments, signaling a potential shift to higher growth markets. The outlook shared for the full year emphasized continued growth in the power systems end market including products for data centers, even as the industrial and transportation demand stays flat.

The Company anticipates strong sales growth of 45% for 2025 compared to 2024, driven by expected growth in the power systems end market including products supporting data centers, while sales in the industrial and transportation end markets are projected to remain flat.

I was even waiting for more confirmation around a net income spike seen in Q2 that was driven by a deferred tax asset release. In Q3, a net income growth of 59% YoY now confirms that operating earnings are indeed improving consistently. The scale of tax related distortion is significantly lower in Q3 ($7m in Q3 versus $29.2m in Q2). The operating leverage trends seen in SG&A expenses have also progressed meaningfully. SG&A rose 39% YoY, well below revenue growth. SG&A as a % of sales declined to 7.5% from 8.7%, implying a meaningful, if not sustained, efficiency gain.

The only drag that has emerged is gross margins, which declined to 23.9% vs 28.9% last year. How much of this is due to a mix shift towards lower margin products remains to be seen because the topline growth has happened in power systems (and data center products). If this is the growth area and comes with lower margins, then the bar for growth in this segment increases to compensate for lower margins. However, if it is primarily due to "temporary inefficiencies from accelerated production ramp", a couple of quarters of watching margins will help ease concerns around pricing power and competitive pressures. In fact, PSIX's competitive positioning was an open concern in September with the likes of Vertiv, Schneider, etc. dominating at scale. That critical part of the thesis has not been helped by the drop in gross margins. Winning demand at lower margins does not yet support a reasonably competitive moat structure.

Roping In Valuations Now

The primary bone of contention in September was valuations. The EV to forward revenues was ~4.35x, which looked too high for an industrials small cap without clear catalysts pointing to a regime change to a data center-led growth. That part of the thesis has altered significantly. Since my September thesis, PSIX valuation multiples have contracted meaningfully and are now ~2.4x - a derating that has worked out faster than I had anticipated.

Combined with growth and income trends and some hints of actual and consistent data center (power systems) led demand, the valuation reset looks like a compelling opportunity for fresh capital deployment in PSIX now. The gap with Vertiv is also palpable. In September, I had noted the EV to forward revenues for Vertiv at ~5.5x - it is now above ~6x. The gap has widened to account for the 10x revenue scale in Vertiv and a somewhat proven competitive position.

Turning to my DCF analysis, I had found a fair value of closer to $55 accounting for a scenario of revenue growth starting from ~30% growth levels and slowing towards 8% (at a scale of ~$1.3b) in 5 years. Coincidentally, those were the same levels from where PSIX found support and has rebounded from recently. The outlook for 2025 is ~45% growth now, and the past two blockbuster quarters have even shown growth north of 60% to make those assumptions more than achievable.

On the other hand, the FCF assumptions of 12% in Year 1 and improving incrementally every year look challenged now, as production ramp phases could drag free cash flow. And more importantly, gross margins are under pressure.

I reran the DCF with an upgrade on growth assumptions, but pulling down FCF assumptions due to the current gross margin concerns. The current range of fair value estimates have improved from the $55 range indicated in September to within striking distance of where we are now.

Turning Into A Cautious Buy

For those who could accumulate PSIX at $50, the risk-reward seems very well-balanced with a reasonable margin for error. At $65-70, the risk reward is less compelling, but close to a Buy. In September, I had mentioned that any drop below $80 would have me take a look at PSIX. That was not to say that $80 would immediately qualify as a Buy - my DCF fair value anchor would have still weighed in. My $80 mark was more indicative of the prevalent market conditions then, where narratives and momentum counted for more and strong rallies in similar stocks were the norm. In those markets, marginal but sustained overvaluation on growth and potential narratives (particularly a data center angle attached) was allowed.

Since then, PSIX has held its end of the bargain by delivering a valuation reset and confirmed growth trends. Therefore, I would overall rate PSIX a cautious Buy. A Buy because of the fundamental progress and valuations closer to a fair value estimate. Cautious because gross margins are still concerning, and the markets overall have changed. Over the past month or two, several narrative-based stocks and AI-adjacent names have corrected heavily. SMCI has corrected ~40%. Some, like Palantir, have rebounded after correcting by over 25%. Each stock has a different story, but markets are no longer looking like the unidirectional run from April to October. Going ahead, narratives and valuations may continue to be challenged, and while markets may reward narratives in pockets, unhindered valuation expansion across the market breadth looks less probable.

Which is why I am issuing a cautious Buy on PSIX despite the stock ticking most of my checklist today (barring margins). I recommend accumulation in PSIX with a view to lapping up some more (fundamentals allowing) if the stock drops further towards the $50-55 mark. Investors will also need to keep an eye on the full-year results to ensure the margin story does not deteriorate further.

data.title: VYM: Real Relative Strength (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Intro & Thesis

The Vanguard High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF Shares (VYM) is an alternative offered by the market for exposure to US large caps, with a marked bias toward the dividends of those companies. To achieve this, this ETF replicates the FTSE High Dividend Yield Index, an index that encompasses a series of companies with high dividend distribution policies.

This index, part of a defined reference universe (stocks of different sizes included in the FTSE family) and uses filtering to eliminate REITs and stocks that do not pay dividends or do not project the payment of dividends at 12 months’ notice. After this, they construct a metric called 12-month forward dividend yield, to sort the companies according to their result. This is in addition to quarterly revisions to update the index if the rules indicate it. All this monitoring is carried out through the “indexation by replication” method, which consists of investing all the fund's assets in the same companies that have the index, with the same weights, in order to achieve a replica as reliable as possible.

From the point of view of the thesis and as we will see below, today I recommend maintaining VYM: the current carry of the ETF is lower than that of lower risk alternatives, so the dividend does not compensate the investor against “risk-free” instruments (see the last paragraph). At the same time, the VYM/SPY relative strength indicator remains positive even with depressed real yields (negative Z-score), suggesting that recent support does not respond to a pure “rate trade”, but to the market’s preference for quality, cash generation, and shareholder returns in a more fragile growth environment. In this context, I would only see a tactical purchase for those who seek to reduce the sensitivity of their portfolio to volatility without leaving equities, assuming that the main risk in the future is a rebound in the premium term. If the long stretch becomes stressed, the opportunity cost of maintaining a vehicle that today offers a significantly lower dividend than lower-risk alternatives increases.

ETF Overview

The table above shows the operational reality and the level of return of the ETF analyzed over time. As the reader will observe, the ETF achieves a more than efficient replica of the benchmark that it aims for, since its returns move practically in parallel (3.46% in the last month; 5.12% in 3 months; 16.20% YTD) and that of the NAV (3.42% last month; 5.12% in 3 months; 16.31% YTD). This fits with the proposed thesis, in the sense that the fund is not properly a carry product, since it is seen that in the long term it has a double-digit historical return and consequently, the argument for maintaining it becomes a profile of companies and relative behavior, not the profitability by dividend itself.

The Portfolio

Analyzing the main features of the ETF portfolio, we can see how it is a very diversified product, with 566 positions in the portfolio today. In terms of quality and valuation, the aggregate metrics are quite reasonable for an ETF of this style. With a PER ratio of 20.4x, a P/B of 2.9x, and a profit growth of 13.7% and a turnover of 13%, the numbers are indicating that this is an ETF that has a low turnover, consistent with the monitoring of the index discussed above.

The sectoral exposure indicates that the fund predominates in the financial sector, with 21% of the equity invested today. This means that we are facing an ETF with sensitivity to the credit cycle in which it will directly influence the future labor and inflationary scenario, since they are the main catalysts to cause stress situations in the credit spreads themselves. The rest of the distribution is also very informative, with a relevant block of Technology (14%), Health (12.8%) and Industrial (12.9%), reinforcing the idea of a fund that bets on quality companies that are influenced by the real economy.

As for the top 10 holdings, they currently represent a combined weight of around 28%, with a significant bias towards the financial sector. This reinforces our idea of maintaining the vehicle, not so much for the carry, but for the companies it owns, which can make money for the reader via capital appreciation.

Payoff Profile

With the dividend card, we reaffirm that VYM is not an aggressive income ETF, but rather a dividend-biased equity ETF with a more moderate payout profile. The TTM dividend yield (2.44%) is below the median for comparable ETFs (2.78%), which fits perfectly with the proposed thesis, as the carry is currently insufficient to compete with lower-risk alternatives, meaning that the dividend is not the central argument for the position. On the positive side is the consistency of the dividend itself, which, as shown in the table, has been paying a growing dividend for 15 consecutive years.

The Peers

Comparing the peers of the VYM, we see that it is not the cheapest ETF, nor is it the largest, nor is it the best performer in all environments, but it is the one that usually fits as a simple core block for those who want dividends without complications. In the comparison table, we can see that VYM shares a very low expense ratio (0.06%) with Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF™ (SCHD), while Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF (VIG) and, above all, Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO) have lower investment costs. If we look at the 1-year total return chart, we can see that VYM, VIG, and VOO are all performing at similar levels of return, which tells me that it makes sense to hold it for the type of exposure it provides, without making it a core part of the portfolio because when the market weighs growth more heavily, VOO tends to outperform VYM.

Final Thoughts

VYM Yield vs Fed Funds & 10 Yr Bond Yield

Real Yield vs Relative Strength (VYM/SPY) - Z - Score

The three metrics above reflect what has been discussed throughout the article. In the first and second metrics, we show the evolution of the yield on the 10-year US bond (red line), current interest rates (EFFR - yellow line), and the evolution of the carry that the vehicle has been paying in recent years. As readers can see, the fund's current carry is 2.4%, while the “risk-free” tranche via EFFR or 10-year bonds is rewarded with 3.64% and 4.12%, respectively. Therefore, it only makes sense to maintain the vehicle as a tactical component if readers are looking for some exposure to mature companies that pay dividends.

The third metric measures the relative strength in z-score of the ETF compared to the real yield of the 10-year bond, in this case acting as a qualitative complement to my analysis. Although real 10-year rates are low today (the “price of money” in real terms is not particularly high), VYM continues to outperform the market when compared to SPY (this is what the orange line of relative strength VYM/SPY measures). This means that the ETF is “beating” the S&P 500 in relative terms, in an environment where, in theory, the growth style should shine more. Therefore, I recommend holding, as the market is relatively rewarding large, mature companies that generate cash today and distribute part of that cash to shareholders (dividends). Thank you for reading.

data.title: GDP Prints To A Huge Upside, But 2026 Remains Unclear
data.description:
NEW YORK (December 23rd)—Gross Domestic Product, or GDP, for the Third Quarter of 2025 (2025Q3) printed this morning at a surprising 4.3%, a full 100 bps above the 3.3% consensus estimate, as well as the Atlanta Fed’s “GDP Now” estimate. The data release was delayed by the October government shutdown. Markets were largely unmoved, as Wall Street typically hedges its holdings and goes largely quiet this close to Christmas.

Real final sales to private domestic purchasers, the sum of consumer spending and gross private fixed investment, increased 3.0%.

The topline print blew away even the upper bound of our highly uncertain 4% estimate of GDP in our November public jobs report. It also exceeded the "Blue Chip" consensus tracked by the St. Louis Fed, which had a wide range of estimates, and the Atlanta Fed’s near real-time GDPNow tracker, which monitors a number of data releases to regularly revise its estimate.

Let's look at our exclusive chart of 2025 Q3 GDP by Major Component.

ANALYSIS & PROGNOSTICATION

This quarter seems to have moved past most of the distortions we saw in Net Exports ("NEX") in 2025 Q1 and Q2 as companies hedged against the Trump Tariffs that took effect in April. As the chart below illustrates, we seem to be back to a "normal" balance of trade in the Third Quarter.

As we explained, companies stocked up with imports prior to what President Trump called “Liberation Day,” the day tariffs were imposed. Since Imports are a reduction in the Net Exports (NEX) calculation, the sharp increase in imports drove the economy into slightly negative GDP in 2025Q1. But then the anomaly reversed in 2025 Q2 as extra inventories burned off and imports were reduced (thereby reducing Gross Domestic Investment from the prior quarter.)

The other components of GDP are largely unremarkable, except that Gross Domestic Investment (GDI) barely moved this quarter. That seems contrary to the news we’ve heard from the Trump Administration about inbound foreign capex investment. That said, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has said we will have a “bountiful” 2026 as policies like the “Big Beautiful Bill” kick in and promised foreign companies' investments come to be realized. New capex also takes a fair amount of time to ramp up, especially for property and plant.

We’re holding to our 3% estimate of GDP for 2025Q4 and 2026Q1, with a “fudge factor” of +/- 50 bps. We’re cautious about that estimate, though, given the anomalies in timely data releases owing to the October shutdown. Watch for our December jobs report in January when the data gets to be a bit clearer.

______________________________

Note: Our commentaries most often tend to be event-driven. They are mostly written from a public policy, economic, or political/geopolitical perspective. Some are written from a management consulting perspective for companies that we believe to be underperforming, and include strategies that we would recommend for the companies of our clients. Others discuss new management strategies we believe will fail. This approach lends special value to contrarian investors to uncover potential opportunities in companies that are otherwise in a downturn. (Opinions here regarding whether to buy, sell, or hold such companies, however, assume the company will not change its current practices.)

data.title: GOEX: A Late-Cycle ETF To Ride Gold
data.description:
If gold is really going through a late-cycle phase, the first instrument that comes to mind is Global X Gold Explorers ETF (GOEX). This represents the segment of gold explorers, which in my opinion is the most speculative tier of the mining segment; not surprisingly, it is known for being a highly cyclical segment and often lacking fundamental values. For this reason it is speculative, therefore tactical, especially in a phase like the current one for gold. But at the same time, in my opinion not strategic at all for a portfolio. What do I mean? Let me explain better. But let's go in order.

What is GOEX?

GOEX is a thematic, commodity-oriented ETF, non-leveraged, focused on gold explorers and developers that replicates the Solactive Global Gold Explorers & Developers Total Return Index.

Participation in this ETF is quite expensive, with an expense ratio of 0.65%, to which is added a 30-day median spread of 0.99%.

Strategy

How is this objective achieved? It starts from an investable universe made up of companies active in the exploration and development phase, not yet in consolidated production, and the index includes companies for which a final investment decision on production has not yet been made (the so-called pre-FID); then a market-cap-weighted weighting is applied. This immediately distinguishes GOEX from classic mining-themed ETFs, therefore from the so-called producers. It should be remembered that producers have revenues, have margins, and offer true operating leverage to the spot price; explorers are not operating businesses, do not pay dividends, and do not have cash flows to discount. Therefore, in my view, the return on explorers depends directly on an increase in optimism, greater risk appetite, and the willingness of others to buy the story at higher prices.

So what does GOEX have that is distinctive in practical terms?

This is a good question. In theory, explorers like GOEX fit in as late-cycle solutions, not early-cycle, precisely because they react to the boom in the price of gold, not with a linear reaction tied to fundamentals. And this is an interesting element as a starting point for the reasoning, because in fact, if you look at it in technical terms, even by widening the time horizon, it is still possible to find a strong parallelism with RING, which instead theoretically has a linear relationship between the price of gold and fundamentals.

Peers

A linear relationship is also present with the junior gold miners segment, a sector with which there should be greater affinity; in this sense, the parallelism with GDXJ is interesting. Here as well, there is a fairly evident positive linear relationship.

And finally, considering a volatility of 33% and its nature, at least in theory, speculative (highly), in my opinion it makes sense to compare it with levered solutions on gold miners. In this sense, the parallelism with GDXU is interesting; the latter wins in terms of performance during bullish cycles but loses over the long run due to the volatility drag typical of 3x leveraged funds.

Holding distribution

The distribution of holdings is fairly fragmented; there are 51 holdings, well distributed, where the top 10 cover about 40% of the total distribution. The search for active solutions in the exploration segment naturally implies a low Weighted Average Market Cap, here equal to $7.3 billion. Not surprisingly, the presence of mid/small-cap miners and explorers such as Hecla, Equinox, Lundin, Alamos, Eldorado, etc., is immediately noticeable. This confirms the nature of GOEX as being more similar to a venture basket than to a hedge on physical gold.

And naturally, this also entails a certain exposure to foreign markets, considering that the core of this segment is not in the US but rather in Canada and Australia, as can be seen from the geographic distribution of the ETF.

My opinion

GOEX seems to be well positioned on a theoretical level in this phase, which in my view can be considered “late cycle.”

The price of gold is at highs, while US inflation is finally starting to ease, partly justifying the last two interest rate cuts by the Federal Reserve. If inflation continues in this direction, real rates could benefit, making the opportunity cost of gold higher.

Although it does not justify a dump in gold, in my view it does justify the time dimension identified as “late cycle.” And the structure of GOEX should theoretically tend to outperform in technical moments like this.

Risk

At this point, I think caution is warranted, and in my view, this makes GOEX suitable for a tactical positioning but not a strategic one within a portfolio. Let me explain better...

  • GOEX is not actually driven directly by the price of gold but by the market’s propensity to speculate on the gold explorers segment.
  • So in my opinion, the performance of this segment depends heavily on risk appetite.
  • If sentiment contracts, for example in a negative phase for gold, appreciation for this segment could disappear entirely.
  • Resulting in a significant underperformance compared to RING or GLD.

In this sense, the sideways phase of gold, from 2013 to 2020, is an interesting case study to better understand the nature of GOEX.

  • In the pre-2017 bull market, the explorers segment benefited from strong capital inflows.
  • Then, in the post-2017 contraction, interest in the segment vanished, and outflows generated a latent, multi-year underperformance, both relative to RING and GLD.

And if history teaches us anything, and it often does… it is also true that in reality, net of a typical outperformance during the upswing cycle, when widening the time horizon, GOEX does not offer such competitive returns.

Conclusion

To conclude, I think that GOEX can be an interesting tactical instrument in certain expansive phases, especially to speculate during late-cycle phases like the one we are potentially experiencing now in gold. At the same time, in my view, one should not make the mistake of thinking that this can be a portfolio substitute, given its speculative nature. For this reason, I believe the appropriate rating for this instrument remains BUY considering the structural momentum, but with a strong focus on a potential reversal, which with GOEX could arrive violently.
